sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation**: no documented path for injuries, billing disputes, safety concerns, or complaints (legal/retention risk)
- **Churn analysis & win-back campaign**: no formal process for tracking why members leave or triggering re-engagement sequences
- **Coach performance review & remediation**: no SOP linking coaching standards (you have 17 coach SOPs but no audit/feedback loop)
- **Financial close & reporting**: no monthly reconciliation, variance review, or cash-flow forecasting documented
- **Health & safety incident log & corrective action**: separate from complaints—equipment failure, falls, accidents need a formal record
- **New coach onboarding checklist & competency sign-off**: you have 17 coach SOPs but no SOP on *how* a new hire learns/proves them
- **Member data privacy & GDPR compliance**: no documented data handling, retention, or breach response
- **Staffing shift coverage & emergency backfill**: no SOP for sick leave, holiday cover, or last-minute cancellations
- **Class cancellation protocol**: who decides, how is it communicated, refund/credit rules?

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding (of your 22)**: likely missing post-Day-1 touchpoints—Week 2 check-in, Month 1 goal review, and habit-stacking cues to reduce early churn
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)**: Email Newsletter & Blog Post are flagged but also missing—social content calendar, community/referral incentive mechanics, and campaign performance review cadence
- **Coach daily/weekly SOPs**: probably don't cover how to *spot* a struggling member mid-class or document progress for retention reporting
- **Facilities SOPs (4/4)**: likely thin on stock/reorder

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

• **Arrest member churn immediately.** June shows 0% attrition (good), but the recent quarter (Apr–Jun) averaged 4.69% vs 3.81% in the prior quarter. May–June lost 5.74% and flatlining at 0%, suggesting volatility. Implement exit interviews, re-engagement campaigns for lapsed members, and a retention bonus (e.g. refer a friend, lock in 12-month deals at 10% discount). Target: drop to <2% baseline.

• **Raise Average Revenue per Member (AR/M) — it's fallen 29% since December (£170 → £120).** You've added 62 members (85 → 147) but revenue flatlined. Pricing hasn't moved with headcount gain. Introduce a tiered membership (£25 core, £35 premium with classes + PT), and push 10% of members to premium by month-end. Test a £5/month price rise on new signups. This alone could add £1,000–£1,500/month at current member count.

• **Fill off-peak class slots with corporate/workplace packages.** May–June revenue stayed flat despite member growth, signalling unutilised capacity in the new larger studio. Sell 5-person corporate memberships to local Belfast firms at £18/head/month (vs £25 individual). Target 3 corporate clients by end of July (£270/month recurring).

## EXPENSES — FOCUS AFTER REVENUE

• **Non-people costs are the real leak post-relocation.** June non-people expenses are £9,390 (53% of revenue). Excluding April's fit-out, the recent quarter averaged £8,054/month vs £4,574 pre-move. That's £3,500/month *structural* overspend. Audit: landlord rent/service charges (likely doubled), utilities, insurance, cleaning. Renegotiate cleaning contracts (in-house vs external), shop around insurance, and confirm rent is competitively priced for the doubled space. Target: cut non-people costs back to £6,500/month (40% of revenue).

• **Payroll (Jun £3,686) is creeping up 10% in six months (May £3,550). You've added 25 members, not 25% more staff.** Confirm headcount hasn't grown; if it has, freeze hiring. If it's wage inflation, cap at 3% annually. June should hold at £3,550 max.

## PROF

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly over-loaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying almost 60% of the team's workload — especially Personal Training (44 sessions) and Body Composition Scans (20). Redistribute 15–20 PT slots to Eilis and Grace to prevent burnout and create capacity for JoJo to focus on the technically demanding Tuesday classes and coaching development.

• **Grace is under-utilised outside Mondays.** She's only doing 52 sessions across the month. Assign her 2–3 additional Elevate or Strongevity slots on Wed/Thu/Fri to leverage her expertise and share the load, while respecting her Monday-only constraint and scaling priorities.

• **Eilis has spare capacity and should absorb more PT and BCS work.** She's only at 48 sessions and is ready to build confidence. Shift 8–10 PT bookings and 5–6 Body Composition Scans her way (her 11 PTs show she can handle it); this frees JoJo and builds Eilis's billable hours.

• **Body Composition Scans are over-reliant on JoJo.** She's doing 20 of them; train Eilis and Grace to deliver these (lower technical bar than PT) so clients have genuine choice and reduce JoJo's session count by ~10 scans.

• **Create a Wednesday or Thursday "Eilis anchor day"** to build her confidence in a non-Tuesday slot and ensure consistent coverage outside peak Monday/Tuesday demand. This stabilises the timetable and prevents single-person dependency on any class.

class_stats.suggestion:
**1. Consolidate low-attendance classes.** Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, Strongevity, and Games Night are consistently underused (12-62 attendees over 90 days). Consider removing or merging these into higher-demand slots, or test repositioning them to Wednesday/Tuesday when overall gym traffic is highest.

**2. Expand Build classes across quieter days.** Your Build: Full Body, Upper, and Lower classes drive 60% of attendance. Schedule additional Build sessions on Monday and Thursday to boost those quiet days, rather than adding niche offerings.

**3. Test a Saturday morning Build class.** Saturday is your weakest day (203 attendees). A single Build class at 9am could capture weekend-focused members and shift Saturday's attendance closer to weekday baseline.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Concrete Steps for Grace

• **Start with your pricing ceiling, not floor.** Interview 3-5 of your best-paying members this week (15 mins each, coffee chat) — ask what they'd *willingly pay more for* and what feels overpriced. This anchors your menu to real willingness-to-pay, not guesswork. Use their language in your descriptions.

• **Create two separate docs immediately.** Coach version: tier structure, what each package includes (classes/PT ratio/cancellation policy/revenue split). Member version: benefit-focused (not internal jargon) — "unlimited studio access + 2 coached sessions/month" not "concurrent booking module." Post the coach version in your staff Slack *now*; don't wait for perfection.

• **Audit what you're actually *selling* vs. offering.** Pull your last 20 member sign-ups — what packages did they choose? What did they ask about that isn't on your current menu? That gap is free revenue. You might be missing a "drop-in class pack" or "nutrition + training bundle" because nobody formally offered it.

• **Run a 48-hour internal feedback sprint.** Send coaches a Loom video explaining the draft menu Friday afternoon. Ask: "Which tier would *you* struggle selling? What objections do you hear?" Implement their feedback by Monday. Coaches are your frontline sales team — they spot problems your spreadsheet won't.

• **Price it slightly aggressive.** You can drop prices later; you can't easily raise them. Leave 10-15% pricing headroom now. Test it for 3 weeks, then adjust based on conversion rates from intro calls.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement — Action Plan for Grace

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first** — Pull analytics from your current pages. Where do visitors leave? What's the last thing they read before bouncing? Start there, not with guessing what "clarity" means. You'll likely find 2-3 specific friction points (pricing buried, no beginner info, unclear class schedule format).

• **Mine your FAQ from real conversations, not assumptions** — Spend 2-3 hours listening to: inquiry emails, Instagram DMs, gym floor objections from trial members. Capture the exact phrasing women use ("Is this intimidating if I've never lifted?" vs. "beginner-friendly atmosphere"). Write your landing copy in *their* language, not marketing-speak.

• **Create a micro-hierarchy showing the three things that matter most** — Women's-only, actual class schedule/times, and one unique differentiator (specific coaching style? community? results timeframe?). Everything else is secondary. Test this by asking: if someone gives you 10 seconds, do they know *exactly* what to do next and why The Forge is different?

• **A/B test the call-to-action friction** — Don't overhaul everything at once. Change one page element (e.g., "Book Free Trial" vs. "Start Today" + swap free trial offer for first-week access). Measure signups for 2 weeks, then iterate.

• **Set a concrete win metric** — Aim for: increase landing page-to-trial conversion by 15% in 4 weeks. If you don't know your current rate, calculate it this week.

What's your current landing page conversion rate (visitors

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Executing Your Feedback System at The Forge

**Start stupidly small — today.** Create a 4-question Google Form (takes 15 mins): "What's working?", "What's frustrating?", "One thing you'd add?", "Refer a friend? (Yes/No)". Link it in your WhatsApp member group, email footer, and print a QR code for the gym wall. Don't overthink this.

**Piggyback on existing moments instead of adding *another* thing.** Post-class is friction — women are sweaty and rushing. Instead: pop a tablet at the front desk during peak hours (evenings/weekends) with the form. Takes 60 seconds. Or embed it in your monthly class schedule email with a "quick 2-min feedback bonus: free smoothie entry" (costs you £2, captures 10x more responses).

**Make feedback *immediately actionable* to prove it matters.** Month 1: collect data. Month 2: implement ONE thing members asked for visibly (new class time? equipment? playlist change?). Announce it in the next email with "You asked, we delivered." This drives 3-4x higher response rates going forward because members see their voice moves needle.

**Assign one person (maybe you) as "feedback owner" with 48-hour response time.** When someone flags an issue, they hear back within 2 days — even if it's just "We're testing this." This builds loyalty faster than perfect execution. It also stops feedback from dying in a spreadsheet.

**Use responses to fuel your referral story.** Every winning insight becomes part of your pitch to new prospects: "Our members told us they needed X,

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for Grace

• **Start with your revenue-heavy services first** — Personal Training & Fuel Forward will show immediate ROI. Nail those two, then roll out the others. Don't try to document everything simultaneously; you'll dilute quality.

• **Interview your best practitioners, not just managers** — Your top PT who gets retention, your most successful Fuel Forward coach. Grab 30-45 mins each, record it, have them walk through a real client journey. This beats generic templates every time. Pay them for the time — it's worth it.

• **Build SOPs around member touchpoints, not internal tasks** — Structure each SOP by "Day 1," "Week 2," "Month 1," "Problem scenarios" (cancellation requests, injury issues, plateaus). Members experience a journey; your ops should mirror that, not your backend.

• **Use your existing wins as the backbone** — You already have clients succeeding in these programs. Ask 2-3 success stories from each service what happened in weeks 2, 4, and 8. Bake their experience into the SOP; it's your proof of concept and your roadmap.

• **Lock each SOP to one owner + deadline** — One person per service "owns" the first draft (due in 2 weeks). Non-negotiable. Then Grace reviews + one round of team feedback. Published, not perfect, beats perfect and unpublished every time.

**What's the bottleneck right now — do you have the people capacity to document, or is it decision fatigue on where to start?**

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Batch-create content in 90-minute sprints every other Sunday** — film 8-12 short clips in one session (member transformations, workout snippets, behind-the-scenes). Use your phone, natural lighting, one backdrop. Schedule them across two weeks in Buffer/Later. This kills the "I don't have time" bottleneck that kills consistency.

• **Build a 4-week rotating content pillar system** — Week 1: Member wins (testimonials, before/afters), Week 2: Educational (form tips, myth-busting), Week 3: Community (events, challenges, staff spotlights), Week 4: Promotional (class promos, referral incentives). Repeat. Your content team (or you) knows exactly what to post, no decision fatigue.

• **Tie posts directly to revenue levers** — Every Wednesday: a class/membership promo. Every Friday: user-generated content (ask members to tag you in gym selfies). Every other Monday: a lead magnet post ("DM for free week trial"). Track which post types drive inquiries via UTM codes or asking new members "where'd you hear about us?"

• **Automate the scheduling tool TODAY** — spend 45 minutes setting up Buffer/Later, add your team/manager as collaborators, set phone notifications for 2 hours before each post goes live (for real-time engagement). Takes 10 minutes/week to manage once live.

• **Measure ruthlessly** — screenshot engagement metrics weekly (likes, comments, clicks, DMs). Drop whatever gets <5% engagement after 3 weeks. Double down on what converts (which posts drive

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the Videography Schedule with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW with a standing calendar invite.** Don't email back-and-forth about dates. Book the next 3 sessions (8, 16, 24 weeks out) with Jordan today—treat it like a non-negotiable client meeting. Confirm a fixed day/time (e.g., "first Tuesday of the cycle").

• **Create a shot list 2 weeks before each session.** Brief Jordan on what content you need: member transformations, class energy, new equipment, testimonials. This isn't about 8 hours of raw footage—it's 90 minutes capturing specific angles/moments you'll actually use on socials and your website.

• **Build a 48-hour post-shoot turnaround for edits.** Frame it with Jordan: you need a 3-5 minute highlight reel + 6-8 short-form clips (60 seconds max) within two days. This keeps momentum and ensures content hits your channels while the session's still fresh in members' minds.

• **Assign ownership internally.** One person (probably you or a staff member) handles scheduling reminders, prepping the gym space, gathering member permission forms, and uploading final videos. Without an owner, it slips.

• **Tie it to a content calendar.** Map which clips go where and when—Instagram Reels week 1, email newsletter week 2, website refresh month 3. Otherwise you'll have great footage sitting unused.

What's your biggest concern blocking this right now?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries

**1. Audit your current leak points first (30 mins, today)**
Map exactly where trial enquiries drop off now—from landing page click → email response → first session → follow-up. You probably have delays between inquiry and first contact (fatal), and no reminder sequence before they forget. Fix the biggest leaks before automating more.

**2. Build a 3-email auto-sequence, not 6**
Day 0 (1hr after enquiry): Welcome + link to book first session + confirm women-only vibe. Day 3: "Haven't booked yet? Here's what to expect" + testimonial from similar-age member. Day 10: Final nudge + limited-time offer or deadline if she's ghosting. Keep it tight—more emails = more unsubscribes.

**3. Lock in the "trial expiry" trigger immediately**
Set a hard automation: if she books but doesn't show up, trigger a 24-hour pre-appointment reminder. If she completes 1-2 sessions, auto-send a "convert to membership" offer on day 35 (before the 6 weeks end) with pricing/payment plan options. This kills your manual follow-up work.

**4. Use Zapier or native platform integrations (not manual CRM work)**
Connect your booking system → email platform → payment tool so nothing requires Grace's hands. Test the full sequence with yourself first; one broken link costs enquiries.

**5. Measure one metric: trial-to-member conversion %**
Track how many 6-week trials convert to paying members *right now*, then rerun the automation after

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist + KPI Ownership — Execute Now

• **Map backwards from revenue.** Don't start with tasks—start with what drives your bottom line (member retention %, new sign-ups, class utilization, revenue per member). Assign each person ONE primary KPI they directly impact. JoJo (ops) owns facility utilization %, Eilis (sales) owns new member conversion, Sarah (retention/community) owns churn rate. Everything else flows from these.

• **Make checklists *constraint-based*, not to-do-based.** Instead of "post on socials," write: "Sarah: 3 posts Wed/Fri by 2pm — measure engagement %. JoJo: equipment maintenance log updated daily — measure downtime incidents." The specificity forces accountability and tracking.

• **Use a simple shared doc (Google Sheet or Notion) with 3 columns only:** Task | Owner | Weekly Target/Metric. Each person can see what others own—prevents overlap and creates friendly visibility pressure. Update it together in a 15-min Friday huddle.

• **Tie ONE financial outcome to each role's checklist.** Example: If JoJo hits 85% class utilization that week, it directly correlates to £X revenue. Make it tangible so they see their effort = money earned.

• **Lock it down by Friday EOD.** Print it, laminate it, put it on the gym wall. Non-negotiable. Review actual results weekly—adjust only if the KPI itself was unrealistic, never because "it was hard."

What's your biggest revenue driver right now—member sign-ups or retention?

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

**Pre-Session (48 hours before):**
- Send each team member a 1-pager WITH their specific role/lane already filled in (don't workshop it live—kills momentum). Frame it as "here's your zone of genius for Q1" not "let's debate this." They'll engage deeper knowing where they stand.

**The 90-minute session flow:**
- **0-15 min:** Grace walks through SSO in conversational language (not corporate speak). Tie it directly to what *they'll actually do*—"This means Sarah runs all retention comms" not abstract mission statements.
- **15-45 min:** Break into pairs by lane (e.g., ops paired with marketing lead). Each pair gets 15 mins to map 3-4 concrete 90-day wins in their zone. They report back 60 seconds each—this forces clarity and ownership.
- **45-75 min:** The brutal bit—call out dependencies and conflicts *right there*. "Marketing needs ops to track X by week 2." Get agreements verbal and noted. Vague handoffs kill momentum.
- **75-90 min:** Each person states back their #1 priority for next 30 days in one sentence. Grace writes them down, shares a photo in team chat after.

**The actual win:**
- Send a follow-up email same day with: SSO, the 90-day priorities by lane, and the 30-day commitments. Make it *their* document they helped build—they'll reference it, not ignore it.

What's the team size? That changes whether you do this all-hands or split across two sessions.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge — 30-Day Execution

**Get the data in 48 hours, not weeks:**
- Have each team member (instructors, admin, personal trainers) log their actual weekly hours across: class delivery, 1-on-1s, admin/cleaning, member onboarding, sales follow-up. Don't estimate—use a simple shared spreadsheet for 3 days this week. You'll spot the stretched ones immediately (anyone hitting 50+ hours or juggling 6+ different tasks daily).

**Identify the actual bottleneck role:**
- You'll likely find one person doing 3 jobs (maybe admin + cleaning + induction classes?). That's your pressure point. Ask Grace: what's generating the most member complaints or slipping? Usually it's sales follow-up or class scheduling—that's the task eating someone alive.

**Rebalance by shifting, not hiring:**
- Don't redistribute evenly—redistribute *by capability*. Move the over-stretched person's weakest task to someone who's underused (e.g., if your admin is drowning in social media, hand it to an instructor with spare capacity). Match it to skills or development goals.

**Lock in accountability for 30 days:**
- Weekly 15-min check-in with each person: "Is this workload sustainable?" If it slips back, you catch it immediately. Most rebalances fail because nobody tracks whether it stuck—make it visible and non-negotiable.

**What's the actual win?** Team retention + capacity to take on 2-3 new high-paying members without burning anyone out.

# Cost Cut Execution for The Forge

**Audit your three biggest expense buckets first — likely staffing, rent, and utilities.**
- Pull last 3 months of bank statements, categorize everything. You'll probably find 60-70% of costs sit in 2-3 areas. Don't waste time squeezing 5% from office supplies.

**For staffing (usually 40-50% of gym costs):**
- Map peak vs. off-peak hours — can you shift to part-time trainers on slow shifts or reduce overlap? Even 2-3 hours/week per staff member compounds fast.
- Renegotiate personal trainer commission structure if applicable — move from % of class fees to flat rate or hybrid.

**For rent/lease:**
- You're unlikely to move, but request a lease review meeting with your landlord armed with comp gym rents in Belfast. Position it as "I want to grow here long-term — help me make this work." Some landlords prefer stability over squeezing lump sums.

**For utilities/services:**
- Switch energy supplier (seriously — 15-20% cuts are common for gyms). Get 3 quotes this week.
- Renegotiate equipment maintenance contracts — often bundled and overpriced. Shop it around.

**Set a 10% target per category and track weekly** — don't try to find it all at once or you'll miss opportunities.

Which bucket is your biggest spend? That's where the leverage is.

# Execute: Update Monthly Finance KPIs at The Forge

**Here's exactly what to do:**

• **Pick 5 KPIs max, not 10+** — Gross margin (revenue minus direct costs), member acquisition cost (marketing spend ÷ new members), churn rate (lost members ÷ total), cash runway (months of operating costs left), and revenue per member. Anything else is noise that distracts from growth decisions.

• **Build a single-page dashboard in Google Sheets** — Pull actuals yesterday (don't wait for perfect accounting), compare to last month and YTD target, highlight red flags in orange. Share with yourself weekly, not monthly. Monthly reviews are too slow to catch problems.

• **Make ONE decision rule per KPI** — e.g., "If churn hits 8%, we pause new member acquisition and run a retention audit." If acquisition cost exceeds £X, scale back that channel immediately. Otherwise you're just collecting data, not acting.

• **Track what moves the needle on revenue growth** — Your daily member count × average membership fee is your engine. Monitor daily signups, cancellations, and upsell rates (add-ons per member). These three tell you if growth is real or borrowed.

• **Schedule 30 mins every Monday morning** — Review Friday's close numbers, spot trends early, adjust tactics by Wednesday. Grace, this beats a long monthly review when momentum's already shifted.

What's your biggest financial blind spot right now — retention, pricing, or acquisition efficiency?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**Get the numbers locked down THIS WEEK:**
- Pull your last 12 months of bank statements + membership data. Map revenue by: new sign-ups, recurring memberships, class packages, PT sessions, retail. This reveals what's actually driving income (you'll probably find one revenue stream is doing 60%+ of work).

**Build a simple 3-scenario model (not a 50-tab spreadsheet):**
- Best case: 15% member growth + 5% price increase (test this Jan)
- Base case: flat membership + current pricing
- Worst case: 10% churn + zero new sign-ups
- Use the LAST 3 months as your baseline—that's your real trend, not annual averages.

**Focus forecasting on your actual constraints:**
- How many members can your space physically handle before you need a second studio/shift?
- What's your PT capacity limit? (This is often your highest-margin revenue—forecast when you hit 80% utilization)
- Seasonal dip timing (summer holiday exodus?)—budget now for Jan/Feb acquisition spend to offset.

**Run one forward scenario for 18 months only:**
- Ignore 5-year plans. Forecast to when you'll need to make your next big investment (new equipment, staff hire, expansion). That's your real decision point.

**Assign one spreadsheet owner (probably you, initially):**
- Update monthly on the 5th. 15 minutes max. Compare actuals vs. forecast—the gaps tell you what's broken in operations or sales.

What's your highest revenue stream right now?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**1. Strip it down to three numbers per service:**
- Revenue per unit (class pack price ÷ attendees, PT session rate, add-on average sell price)
- Direct costs (instructor pay per class, PT trainer cut, product cost for add-ons)
- Allocation of fixed overhead (rent, utilities split proportionally by revenue %)
→ *Do this in a spreadsheet today. You don't need perfect data—use last month's actuals and extrapolate.*

**2. Surface your real margin winners fast:**
- PT sessions likely have 60-70%+ gross margins (high price, low variable cost)
- Classes probably 20-30% (packed rooms still need one instructor salary)
- Add-ons (protein, merch, bags) are probably 40%+ but low volume
→ *This tells you where to push sales effort. If PT is your goldmine, Grace should be recruiting clients for it, not just offering it.*

**3. Track the hidden profitability killer—cancellations & no-shows:**
- Add a line: "% of bookings that actually happen"
- A 20% no-show rate on a PT session is the difference between profit and loss
→ *Implement a text reminder system this week. Cuts no-shows by 30-40% with zero cost.*

**4. Test pricing fast with the data:**
- Once you see margins, you'll know if a class price increase from £6 to £7 adds profit or kills attendance
- Use 2-week test cohorts on new pricing, track per-service revenue before/after
→ *Don't wait

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automation Stack — Here's How Grace Should Execute This

• **Pull a real enquiry through the full funnel TODAY.** Don't audit from a desk — go through every step as a cold prospect yourself. You'll spot breakdowns (missing emails, wrong trial slot triggers, dead links) that spreadsheets hide. Time this: 2 hours max, do it before anything else.

• **Export your automation logs (Zapier/Make/whatever you use) and sort by frequency.** Look for duplicate triggers firing the same action — common culprit is "new form submission" + "new contact created" both sending welcome emails. Kill the redundant one. Takes 30 mins if you're organized.

• **Identify the actual conversion bottleneck in YOUR numbers.** Is it enquiry-to-response lag? Trial-to-membership conversion? Fix the one costing you money first, not every automation equally. If trials are booking but members aren't converting, your problem isn't the enquiry flow — it's post-trial.

• **Set one person as "automation owner"** (even if it's you for now) with monthly audit calendar reminders. Automations decay — integrations break, email providers change rules. One person accountable = it stays fixed.

• **Document the actual working flow** (screenshot each step, note which tool does what). This becomes your repeatable system when you hire someone to manage it later.

**What's the biggest leak in your current flow — do you know where enquiries are dropping off?**

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation - Concrete Execution for The Forge

**Identify your 3 tasks NOW (pick from these high-ROI candidates):**
- **Email onboarding sequences** – New members get identical welcome, class schedule, and payment info emails. Build a simple Zapier workflow (Gmail → template responses) that triggers when someone completes signup. Saves 10-15 mins/week but more importantly, ensures consistency.
- **Class cancellation notices** – When Grace cancels a session, manually texting/emailing 20+ members is brutal. Use Twilio via Zapier to auto-send SMS the moment she updates your class calendar. One toggle = done.
- **Monthly renewal reminders** – 3 days before membership expires, send a personalized email. Create this in Mailchimp with a simple automation rule. It'll recover at least 1-2 memberships/month you'd otherwise lose.

**Execution sequence (this week):**
1. Pick ONE task (class cancellations – highest pain). Spend 90 mins building the Zapier workflow. Cost = free or £20/month depending on volume.
2. Test it live on a dummy class cancellation. Iterate for 2 days.
3. Add task #2 (renewal reminders) next week once you've proven the system works.

**The real gain:** You're not saving Grace hours – you're removing decision friction so renewals don't slip and cancellations don't create member frustration. That's retention, not efficiency.

Which of these three resonates most with your current pain point?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security Action Plan for The Forge Website

• **Audit first, buy second** – Use free tools (SSL Labs, GTmetrix) to identify actual vulnerabilities before spending money. Check: SSL certificate status, outdated plugins, weak admin access. This takes 2 hours and tells you what's genuinely broken vs. nice-to-have.

• **Hire a local WordPress specialist for 1-2 days, not an agency retainer** – Contact Belfast dev shops or Upwork for someone to install security plugins (Wordfence or Sucuri), force HTTPS, add 2FA to admin login, and set up automated backups to cloud storage. ~£400-600 fixes 80% of real risk. DIY guides will cost you more in lost time.

• **Password & access overhaul this week** – Change all admin passwords to 16+ characters, remove any staff with old login access, create a shared vault (Bitwarden/1Password) for your team. One weak password from a departing trainer exposes member data; this is your real exposure point.

• **Set a specific monthly maintenance rule** – Schedule plugin updates on the first Monday of each month (30 mins). Member data breach = reputational death for a women's gym; automated updates prevent 90% of exploits that hackers actually use.

• **Get cyber liability insurance quote** – Costs ~£300-500/year for a small gym. Covers legal costs if member data leaks. Do this *after* the above fixes—premiums are lower when you've documented security measures.

What's your current website platform?

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

• Your cost-per-lead has tripled in two months (£6 → £20) while lead volume collapsed 70% (69 → 21). This screams audience fatigue and/or creative decay—you're showing the same ads to the same women repeatedly, so click-through rates have tanked and Meta's algorithm is throttling your reach.

• Your close rate has halved (29% in April → 48% in June on raw numbers, but 23 sales from 38 leads in May vs 10 from 21 in June = 61% → 48%). You're getting fewer, lower-intent leads. The quality drop + quantity drop suggests your creative isn't filtering for genuine fitness interest anymore; you're just paying to reach ad-fatigued lookalikes.

• Profitability is still positive (£1538 in June) but you're burning cash inefficiently. At £20/lead with a 48% close rate, you need £41.67 revenue per lead just to break even on ad spend alone—you're making it work on volume and a strong product, not a strong campaign.

**Actions**

1. **Pause and refresh creative immediately.** You're running the same ad(s) for 6+ weeks. Test 3 completely new angles next month: (a) a "real member transformation" 30-sec video testimonial, (b) a pain-point ad ("tired of mixed gyms? Here's why women-only changes everything"), (c) a FOMO/scarcity angle ("6-week trial closes 15th of each month—only 8 spots left"). Pause your top-performing April creative entirely.

2. **Narrow your audience ruthlessly.** Don't just reuse your April audience. Layer in a custom audience of women aged 28–45 in Belfast postcodes BT5-BT17 who've engaged with fitness/wellness content in the past 90 days, *exclude* anyone who's already clicked your ads. You'll get fewer leads initially, but cost-per-lead should drop back to £8–12 and close rate will rise because you're hitting fresh, intent-rich prospects.

3. **Test a smaller, hyper-targeted spend
